title: Title: Diltiazem vs. Glyceryl Trinitrate: Rethinking First-Line Therapy for Chronic Anal Fissure
text:
Title: Diltiazem vs. Glyceryl Trinitrate: Rethinking First-Line Therapy for Chronic Anal Fissure

Introduction
The management of chronic anal fissure remains a clinical challenge, with topical therapies offering a non-surgical alternative to sphincterotomy. A recent randomised controlled trial by Aggarwal et al. (2015) directly compared 0.2% glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) ointment with 2% diltiazem ointment in 60 patients, finding comparable healing rates but a more favourable side-effect profile for diltiazem. This raises an important question: should diltiazem replace GTN as first-line medical therapy for chronic anal fissure in UK practice?

The Evidence
Aggarwal et al.'s study randomised 60 patients with chronic anal fissure into three groups: topical 0.2% GTN twice daily, topical 2% diltiazem three times daily, or conservative management. After eight weeks, healing rates were 78.6% for GTN, 92.3% for diltiazem, and 46.2% for controls. Pain scores improved significantly in both treatment groups compared to controls, with diltiazem showing earlier pain relief. Crucially, headache—the most common adverse effect of GTN—occurred in 10% of the GTN group but was absent in the diltiazem and control groups. No cases of perianal dermatitis were reported in any group.

Clinical Perspective
These findings align with previous studies demonstrating similar efficacy between calcium channel blockers and nitrates for chronic anal fissure. The mechanism of action differs: GTN works through nitric oxide-mediated smooth muscle relaxation, while diltiazem inhibits calcium influx, reducing internal anal sphincter tone. Both approaches address the fundamental pathophysiology of chronic fissure—persistent sphincter hypertonia and ischaemia.

The clinical significance lies in the side-effect profile. Headaches affect up to 60% of patients using topical GTN, often leading to treatment discontinuation. Diltiazem's superior tolerability could translate to better treatment adherence and higher healing rates in real-world settings. The study's finding of earlier pain relief with diltiazem is also clinically relevant, as rapid symptom improvement improves patient quality of life and treatment satisfaction.

However, several considerations merit attention. The study's sample size (20 patients per group) limits statistical power, particularly for subgroup analyses. The control group's 46% healing rate suggests many chronic fissures may heal with conservative measures alone, though this is higher than typically reported in the literature. Additionally, the study excluded patients with inflammatory bowel disease or other complicating conditions, limiting generalisability to complex cases.

Wider Implications
If replicated in larger studies, these findings could influence UK treatment guidelines. Current British Society of Gastroenterology guidance recommends either topical GTN or diltiazem as first-line therapy, with surgery reserved for refractory cases. Aggarwal et al.'s results suggest diltiazem might be preferred due to better tolerability, potentially improving treatment uptake and completion rates.

From a healthcare economics perspective, improved tolerability could reduce consultation rates for side-effect management and treatment discontinuation. This aligns with the NHS's focus on patient-centred care and reducing treatment burden. However, cost considerations matter—whilst both medications are relatively inexpensive, any price differential could influence prescribing patterns.

The study also highlights the need for personalised medicine approaches. Some patients may still prefer GTN if they've had success previously, or if they have contraindications to calcium channel blockers. Shared decision-making, discussing the trade-off between slightly slower initial pain relief with potentially better overall tolerability, becomes essential.

Conclusion
Aggarwal et al.'s study provides compelling evidence that 2% diltiazem ointment offers comparable efficacy to 0.2% GTN for chronic anal fissure, with a more favourable side-effect profile and earlier pain relief. Whilst larger confirmatory studies are needed, these findings suggest diltiazem deserves serious consideration as first-line medical therapy. The question now is not whether these medications work, but which offers the best balance of efficacy, tolerability, and patient acceptability in routine clinical practice.

The views expressed in this Op-Ed reflect a commentary on published evidence and do not constitute clinical advice or treatment recommendations.

title: Title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Disease: From Nutrient to Pharmacological Agent
text:
Title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Disease: From Nutrient to Pharmacological Agent

Introduction
The anti-inflammatory potential of omega-3 fatty acids has been a subject of intense research for decades, yet clinical translation remains inconsistent. Dr Philip Calder's comprehensive analysis of omega-3 mechanisms and clinical evidence provides a framework for understanding when and how these fatty acids might benefit patients with inflammatory conditions. The central tension is clear: whilst preclinical models consistently demonstrate anti-inflammatory effects, human trials for conditions like IBD and asthma have produced mixed results. This discrepancy demands careful examination of dosage, patient selection, and biological monitoring.

The Evidence
Dr Calder's work systematically outlines four core anti-inflammatory mechanisms through which EPA and DHA operate. These include membrane remodelling (displacing pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid), eicosanoid competition (producing less potent inflammatory mediators), gene expression modulation (inhibiting NF-κB activation), and GPR120 receptor activation on macrophages. Crucially, he emphasises that omega-3s don't merely suppress inflammation but actively resolve it through specialised pro-resolving mediators (SPMs) including resolvins, protectins, and maresins.

The clinical evidence shows the strongest support for rheumatoid arthritis, where meta-analyses of 17 RCTs confirm significant reductions in joint tenderness, morning stiffness, and pain scores. The REDUCE-IT trial (n=8,179) demonstrated a 25% reduction in major cardiovascular events with 4g/day EPA. However, trials in IBD and asthma have been inconsistent, with human studies failing to replicate the robust benefits seen in animal models.

Clinical Perspective
The key insight from Calder's analysis is that dosage fundamentally determines whether omega-3s function as nutrients or pharmacological agents. Standard Western dietary intake (250-500mg/day) provides essential fatty acids for normal physiological function but falls far short of the >2,000mg/day threshold required for measurable anti-inflammatory effects. This explains why many clinical trials using standard supplement doses have failed to demonstrate benefit.

The concept of the Omega-3 Index as a biomarker represents a paradigm shift. Rather than assuming all patients have similar baseline omega-3 status, this index (measuring EPA+DHA in red blood cell membranes) allows personalised assessment and dosing. A target of ≥8% is associated with optimal cardiovascular protection and anti-inflammatory effects, yet most Western populations sit at 4-5%.

For IBD specifically, the inconsistent trial results likely reflect methodological shortcomings. Many studies have failed to account for baseline omega-3 status, used insufficient doses, or included heterogeneous patient populations with varying disease activity. The biological rationale remains strong - reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine production and enhanced SPM formation should theoretically benefit IBD patients. However, without proper patient stratification and dose optimisation, these benefits remain elusive.

Wider Implications
The transition of omega-3s from nutritional supplements to pharmacological agents has significant implications for UK healthcare. First, it challenges the current regulatory framework that treats all omega-3 products similarly regardless of concentration or intended use. Prescription-grade formulations delivering 2-4g/day EPA/DHA represent a distinct therapeutic category requiring different prescribing and monitoring protocols.

Second, the Omega-3 Index provides an opportunity for personalised medicine in inflammatory disease management. Rather than adopting a one-size-fits-all approach, clinicians could measure baseline status and titrate doses to achieve therapeutic targets. This mirrors established practices in other therapeutic areas where drug monitoring guides treatment.

Third, the evidence supports reconsidering omega-3s as adjunctive therapy in high-risk cardiovascular patients with inflammatory conditions. The dual benefit of triglyceride reduction and anti-inflammatory effects could provide additive protection beyond standard therapies.

Conclusion
Dr Calder's analysis makes clear that omega-3 fatty acids possess genuine anti-inflammatory potential, but realising this benefit requires treating them as pharmacological agents rather than simple supplements. The threshold for anti-inflammatory effects (>2g/day EPA/DHA), the need for biological monitoring (Omega-3 Index ≥8%), and the recognition of individual baseline status are all critical factors that have been overlooked in many clinical trials. For UK clinicians managing inflammatory conditions, the question is no longer whether omega-3s work, but rather how to identify the patients most likely to benefit and implement appropriate dosing and monitoring protocols. The evidence suggests that with proper application, these fatty acids could provide meaningful adjunctive therapy for selected patients with inflammatory diseases.

References
01 Calder PC. Omega-3 fatty acids and inflammatory processes: from molecules to man. Biochem Soc Trans. 2017;45(5):1105-1115.
02 Calder PC. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and inflammatory processes: nutrition or pharmacology? Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2013;75(3):645-662.
03 Bhatt DL, et al. Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Icosapent Ethyl for Hypertriglyceridemia. N Engl J Med. 2019;380:11-22.
04 Serhan CN. Pro-resolving lipid mediators are leads for resolution physiology. Nature. 2014;510:92-101.
05 Djuricic I, Calder PC. n-3 Fatty Acids (EPA and DHA) and Cardiovascular Health. Nutrients. 2021;13(7):2019.

title: Title: Metronidazole and GTN: A New Standard for Acute Anal Fissure Treatment?
text:
Title: Metronidazole and GTN: A New Standard for Acute Anal Fissure Treatment?

Introduction
The management of acute anal fissure remains a clinical challenge, with conventional treatments often falling short of optimal outcomes. A recent randomised controlled trial by Elgendy et al. (2024) suggests that adding topical metronidazole to standard glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) therapy significantly improves healing rates and reduces pain in acute anal fissures. This finding challenges the current conservative management paradigm and warrants careful consideration of whether metronidazole should become standard adjunctive therapy.

The Evidence
Elgendy and colleagues conducted a single-blinded RCT involving 100 patients with acute anal fissures, randomly assigning them to either GTN 0.2% alone or GTN 0.2% combined with metronidazole 10% topical cream. The primary outcomes were fissure healing and pain reduction measured by visual analogue scale (VAS).

After one month of treatment, the combination therapy group demonstrated superior outcomes: 96% complete healing versus 86% in the GTN-only group (p=0.004). Pain scores showed similar improvement, with VAS dropping from 8 to 2 in the combination group compared to 8 to 3 in the GTN-only group (p<0.001). The combination therapy also achieved faster healing, with 50% of patients healing within 11-20 days compared to only 24% in the control group.

Clinical Perspective
These findings are compelling but require contextual interpretation. The study's strengths include appropriate randomisation, adequate sample size for detecting clinically meaningful differences, and standardised outcome measures. However, the single-centre design and relatively short follow-up period limit generalisability.

The proposed mechanism—that metronidazole's antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties address bacterial colonisation that impedes healing—aligns with emerging understanding of anal fissure pathophysiology. Previous research has demonstrated anaerobic bacterial presence in approximately 50% of anal fissures, supporting the rationale for targeted antimicrobial therapy.

However, several considerations temper enthusiasm for immediate practice change. The study excluded patients with chronic fissures, those with inflammatory bowel disease, and pregnant women, limiting applicability to these common clinical scenarios. Additionally, the potential for antimicrobial resistance development with widespread metronidazole use cannot be ignored.

Wider Implications
If validated in larger, multi-centre trials with longer follow-up, this approach could significantly impact anal fissure management. The combination therapy offers a genuinely conservative alternative to surgical intervention, potentially reducing healthcare costs and patient morbidity. For UK practice, this could mean fewer referrals to colorectal surgeons and reduced waiting times for definitive treatment.

The findings also highlight the importance of reconsidering the role of infection in anorectal disorders more broadly. If bacterial colonisation significantly impacts healing in anal fissures, similar mechanisms might operate in other conditions like haemorrhoids or postoperative wound complications.

Conclusion
The evidence from Elgendy et al. (2024) presents a strong case for incorporating topical metronidazole into acute anal fissure management protocols. While not yet sufficient to mandate practice change, these findings justify a carefully designed multi-centre trial to confirm efficacy and establish optimal treatment duration. Until then, clinicians should consider this combination therapy for patients with acute fissures failing to respond to conventional treatment, particularly those with evidence of infection or inflammation.

The question now is not whether metronidazole helps, but how we should integrate this evidence into practice while awaiting definitive confirmation of its role in anal fissure management.

References
Elgendy HM, AbdelMawla A, Hussein AF. Efficacy of local metronidazole with glyceryl trinitrate versus topical glyceryl trinitrate alone in the treatment of acute anal fissure: a randomized clinical trial. The Egyptian Journal of Surgery. 2024;43:304-308. doi:10.4103/ejs.ejs_284_23

The views expressed in this Op-Ed reflect a commentary on published evidence and do not constitute clinical advice or treatment recommendations.

title: Title: A New Classification System for Perianal Fistulising Crohn’s Disease: A Step Towards Personalised Management
text:
Title: A New Classification System for Perianal Fistulising Crohn’s Disease: A Step Towards Personalised Management

Introduction
Perianal fistulising Crohn’s disease represents one of the most challenging phenotypes in inflammatory bowel disease management. Despite advances in biological therapies, patients continue to experience significant morbidity, with many requiring defunctioning stomas or proctectomy. The absence of a comprehensive classification system has hindered both clinical decision-making and research efforts. In this commentary, we examine a new expert consensus classification system that aims to address these limitations by incorporating patient goals, disease severity, and treatment outcomes into a flexible framework.

The Evidence
The proposed classification system, developed through a modified nominal group technique involving international experts, identifies four distinct classes of perianal fistulising Crohn’s disease (Geldof et al., 2022). Class 1 represents minimal disease requiring minimal intervention, while Class 2 encompasses chronic symptomatic fistulae that can be further subdivided into those suitable for repair (2a), requiring symptom control (2b), or representing early rapidly progressive (2c-i) or gradually debilitating disease (2c-ii). Class 3 includes severe disease requiring proctectomy, and Class 4 addresses persistent perineal symptoms following proctectomy.

Clinical Perspective
This classification represents a significant advancement over previous systems that focused primarily on fistula morphology and anatomy. By incorporating patient goals and disease outcomes, it acknowledges the heterogeneous nature of perianal fistulising Crohn’s disease and the need for personalised management strategies. The flexibility to cycle between classes over time reflects the dynamic nature of the disease and allows for treatment escalation or de-escalation based on response.

The emphasis on synchronising patient and clinician goals is particularly noteworthy. The PISA trial demonstrated that patient satisfaction depends on whether interventions align with individual goals, with those choosing symptom control through seton drainage reporting higher satisfaction than those randomised to surgical repair despite similar clinical outcomes (Wasmann et al., 2020). This patient-centred approach represents a paradigm shift in managing complex perianal disease.

Wider Implications
The classification system has significant implications for both clinical practice and research. By providing clear guidance on treatment strategies for each class, it facilitates multidisciplinary decision-making and helps identify patients who may benefit from early referral to specialist centres. The system also addresses the unmet need for indications for defunctioning surgery and proctectomy, potentially allowing for earlier intervention in appropriate patients.

For research purposes, the classification enables more precise patient stratification in clinical trials. Different classes may be suitable for different trial designs, with Class 2a patients potentially appropriate for studies of fistula closure interventions, while Class 2b patients might be better suited to trials of symptom control or quality-of-life interventions. This targeted approach could improve trial efficiency and enhance our understanding of treatment effectiveness across the disease spectrum.

Conclusion
The new classification system for perianal fistulising Crohn’s disease represents an important step towards personalised management of this challenging condition. By incorporating disease severity, patient goals, and treatment outcomes into a flexible framework, it provides practical guidance for clinical decision-making while facilitating more targeted research efforts. Future work should focus on validating the system in clinical practice and refining definitions of key concepts such as optimised medical therapy and suitability for surgical repair. As our biological understanding of perianal fistulising Crohn’s disease improves, this classification may evolve into a more biologically-based system, but for now, it provides a much-needed framework for managing this complex disease phenotype.

The question now facing the field is how to implement this classification in routine clinical practice and whether it will indeed lead to improved patient outcomes and satisfaction. Validation studies and prospective implementation research will be crucial in answering these questions and refining the system for optimal clinical utility.

References
Geldof J, Iqbal N, LeBlanc J-F, et al. Classifying perianal fistulising Crohn’s disease: an expert consensus to guide decision-making in daily practice and clinical trials. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2022;7(6):576-584.

Wasmann KATGM, de Groof EJ, Stellingwerf ME, et al. Treatment of perianal fistulas in Crohn’s disease, seton versus anti-TNF versus surgical closure following anti-TNF (PISA): a randomised controlled trial. J Crohns Colitis. 2020;14(7):1049-1056.

The views expressed in this Op-Ed reflect a commentary on published evidence and do not constitute clinical advice or treatment recommendations.

title: Title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Disease: From Nutrient to Pharmacological Agent
text:
Title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Disease: From Nutrient to Pharmacological Agent

Introduction
The anti-inflammatory potential of omega-3 fatty acids has been a subject of intense research for decades, yet clinical translation remains inconsistent. Dr Philip Calder's comprehensive analysis of omega-3 mechanisms and clinical evidence provides a framework for understanding when and how these fatty acids might benefit patients with inflammatory conditions. The central tension is clear: whilst preclinical models consistently demonstrate anti-inflammatory effects, human trials for conditions like IBD and asthma have produced mixed results. This discrepancy demands careful examination of dosage, patient selection, and biological monitoring.

The Evidence
Dr Calder's work systematically outlines four core anti-inflammatory mechanisms through which EPA and DHA operate. These include membrane remodelling (displacing pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid), eicosanoid competition (producing less potent inflammatory mediators), gene expression modulation (inhibiting NF-κB activation), and GPR120 receptor activation on macrophages. Crucially, he emphasises that omega-3s don't merely suppress inflammation but actively resolve it through specialised pro-resolving mediators (SPMs) including resolvins, protectins, and maresins.

The clinical evidence shows the strongest support for rheumatoid arthritis, where meta-analyses of 17 RCTs confirm significant reductions in joint tenderness, morning stiffness, and pain scores. The REDUCE-IT trial (n=8,179) demonstrated a 25% reduction in major cardiovascular events with 4g/day EPA. However, trials in IBD and asthma have been inconsistent, with human studies failing to replicate the robust benefits seen in animal models.

Clinical Perspective
The key insight from Calder's analysis is that dosage fundamentally determines whether omega-3s function as nutrients or pharmacological agents. Standard Western dietary intake (250-500mg/day) provides essential fatty acids for normal physiological function but falls far short of the >2,000mg/day threshold required for measurable anti-inflammatory effects. This explains why many clinical trials using standard supplement doses have failed to demonstrate benefit.

The concept of the Omega-3 Index as a biomarker represents a paradigm shift. Rather than assuming all patients have similar baseline omega-3 status, this index (measuring EPA+DHA in red blood cell membranes) allows personalised assessment and dosing. A target of ≥8% is associated with optimal cardiovascular protection and anti-inflammatory effects, yet most Western populations sit at 4-5%.

For IBD specifically, the inconsistent trial results likely reflect methodological shortcomings. Many studies have failed to account for baseline omega-3 status, used insufficient doses, or included heterogeneous patient populations with varying disease activity. The biological rationale remains strong - reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine production and enhanced SPM formation should theoretically benefit IBD patients. However, without proper patient stratification and dose optimisation, these benefits remain elusive.

Wider Implications
The transition of omega-3s from nutritional supplements to pharmacological agents has significant implications for UK healthcare. First, it challenges the current regulatory framework that treats all omega-3 products similarly regardless of concentration or intended use. Prescription-grade formulations delivering 2-4g/day EPA/DHA represent a distinct therapeutic category requiring different prescribing and monitoring protocols.

Second, the Omega-3 Index provides an opportunity for personalised medicine in inflammatory disease management. Rather than adopting a one-size-fits-all approach, clinicians could measure baseline status and titrate doses to achieve therapeutic targets. This mirrors established practices in other therapeutic areas where drug monitoring guides treatment.

Third, the evidence supports reconsidering omega-3s as adjunctive therapy in high-risk cardiovascular patients with inflammatory conditions. The dual benefit of triglyceride reduction and anti-inflammatory effects could provide additive protection beyond standard therapies.

Conclusion
Dr Calder's analysis makes clear that omega-3 fatty acids possess genuine anti-inflammatory potential, but realising this benefit requires treating them as pharmacological agents rather than simple supplements. The threshold for anti-inflammatory effects (>2g/day EPA/DHA), the need for biological monitoring (Omega-3 Index ≥8%), and the recognition of individual baseline status are all critical factors that have been overlooked in many clinical trials. For UK clinicians managing inflammatory conditions, the question is no longer whether omega-3s work, but rather how to identify the patients most likely to benefit and implement appropriate dosing and monitoring protocols. The evidence suggests that with proper application, these fatty acids could provide meaningful adjunctive therapy for selected patients with inflammatory diseases.

References
01 Calder PC. Omega-3 fatty acids and inflammatory processes: from molecules to man. Biochem Soc Trans. 2017;45(5):1105-1115.
02 Calder PC. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and inflammatory processes: nutrition or pharmacology? Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2013;75(3):645-662.
03 Bhatt DL, et al. Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Icosapent Ethyl for Hypertriglyceridemia. N Engl J Med. 2019;380:11-22.
04 Serhan CN. Pro-resolving lipid mediators are leads for resolution physiology. Nature. 2014;510:92-101.
05 Djuricic I, Calder PC. n-3 Fatty Acids (EPA and DHA) and Cardiovascular Health. Nutrients. 2021;13(7):2019.

title: Header: Scientific Publication Summary:
text:
# Header: Scientific Publication Summary:
# Title: Patient Perspectives on Nutrition

Authors: European Patients' Forum (EPF), European Genetic Alliances Network (EGAN), European Nutrition for Health Alliance (ENHA)
Journal: Not applicable (booklet)
Year: 2013
Volume/Pages: Not applicable
DOI: Not applicable

---

## Background & Rationale

This booklet addresses the critical role of nutrition in both health maintenance and disease management, focusing on the challenges faced by individuals with chronic conditions who struggle to achieve adequate nutrition despite making healthy dietary choices. The publication emerged from a 2012 conference on "The relevance of nutrition for European patient groups" that highlighted the burden of malnutrition on healthcare systems due to poor identification of nutritional risk and lack of inclusion of nutritional care plans in disease management. The booklet aims to bridge the knowledge gap between nutritional science and patient experiences, providing a comprehensive resource that combines expert scientific perspectives with real-world patient insights.

## Study Design

This is not a clinical trial but rather a collaborative educational publication produced by three European organizations: the European Patients' Forum (EPF), the European Genetic Alliances Network (EGAN), and the European Nutrition for Health Alliance (ENHA). The booklet is structured in two parts: Part 1 was written by nutritional scientist Dr. Ceri Green and covers the scientific principles of nutrition and disease management, while Part 2 contains contributions from various European patient groups describing their specific nutritional challenges. The publication was supported by an unrestricted educational grant from ENHA and was first presented during the 10th Annual Meeting of the European Patients' Forum in Dublin in May 2013.

## Patient Population

The booklet addresses a broad spectrum of patient populations across different chronic conditions and life stages. It covers nutritional needs from preconception through older age, with specific focus on individuals with conditions such as phenylketonuria (PKU), inflammatory bowel disease, coeliac disease, cancer, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), cystic fibrosis, cerebral palsy, and various neurological disorders. The publication also addresses special populations including extremely low birth weight preterm infants, older adults in postoperative care, and individuals with swallowing difficulties or gastrointestinal obstructions.

## Key Findings

The booklet presents several key findings regarding nutrition and disease management. It emphasizes that malnutrition encompasses both undernutrition and overnutrition, with disease-related undernutrition being particularly problematic. The publication highlights that even overweight individuals can suffer from insufficient nutritional intake due to various medical conditions. It identifies critical periods when adequate nutrition is especially important, including preconception, pregnancy, early childhood, older age, and during acute or chronic illness. The booklet explains that specialized nutritional products may be needed temporarily or permanently for certain conditions, including oral nutritional supplements, enteral nutrition via tube feeding, and parenteral nutrition administered intravenously. It also discusses the economic burden of malnutrition on healthcare systems, citing studies showing costs amounting to billions of euros across European countries.

## Discussion

The booklet discusses the importance of a holistic approach to nutritional care that involves governments, policymakers, healthcare professionals, patients, and carers. It emphasizes the need for greater awareness about ensuring adequate nutritional status for all individuals, particularly those with compromised health situations. The publication addresses the regulatory frameworks applicable to medical nutrition, noting that enteral nutrition falls under food legislation while parenteral nutrition is regulated as a pharmaceutical product. It also discusses the benefits of medical nutrition for both patients and healthcare systems, including improved functional and clinical outcomes, reduced complications, and potential cost savings. The booklet highlights the importance of screening programs to identify individuals at risk of malnutrition and the need for regular monitoring throughout a patient's healthcare journey.

## Authors' Conclusions

The authors conclude that high-quality nutrition in general and medical nutrition in particular have been shown to be beneficial to patients and healthcare systems. They emphasize that sound nutritional principles followed throughout the lifespan can help avoid certain nutrition-related chronic conditions that currently pose a huge burden on health systems. The booklet calls for increased public awareness about the role of nutrition in both health and disease management, and stresses the importance of adopting a holistic approach where all stakeholders understand their role in ensuring good nutritional status. The authors advocate for breaking down silos and creating joined-up approaches across healthcare settings and communities to reduce the existing knowledge gap around malnutrition and medical nutrition. They conclude that screening for nutritional risk is the first step toward better inclusion of nutrition in disease management, leading to improved patient outcomes.

Not reported in the source publication.
